findings.title: Content-Security-Policy Header Omitted
findings.observation: No Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header was observed on the response.
findings.aiAnalysis: No CSP header was observed on the scanned response. However, no active XSS exploit vector was successfully demonstrated. The finding is treated as a best-practice hardening recommendation rather than evidence of exploitable risk.
findings.falsePositiveAssessment: A missing CSP header is a common observation. Modern client-side frameworks and CDNs may mitigate this. In the absence of demonstrated XSS, this remains a hardening advisory. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
findings.businessImpact: Lack of CSP increases the impact of future script injection (XSS) bugs, allowing session hijacking.
findings.remediation: Implement a strict Content-Security-Policy header, setting default-src to self and whitelisting trusted hosts.

findings.description: No Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header was observed on the response.
findings.reasoning: No CSP header was observed on the scanned response. However, no active XSS exploit vector was successfully demonstrated. The finding is treated as a best-practice hardening recommendation rather than evidence of exploitable risk.

findings.title: Missing X-Content-Type-Options Header
findings.observation: The X-Content-Type-Options header is missing, allowing browsers to MIME-sniff response content types.
findings.aiAnalysis: No X-Content-Type-Options header was observed. Browsers may attempt to parse files (like images or user uploads) as JavaScript, presenting an XSS vector if file upload controls are weak.
findings.falsePositiveAssessment: Without a file upload vector or injection sink, MIME sniffing does not present a direct exploit path. Downgraded to Best Practice. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
findings.businessImpact: Low. Only creates risk if users can upload arbitrary files that the browser might parse as script.
findings.remediation: Configure your web server to return X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff for all HTTP responses.

findings.title: Cookie Missing HttpOnly Flag: __Secure-STRP
findings.observation: The cookie "__Secure-STRP" (Session Identifier) is not protected with the HttpOnly attribute, allowing client-side scripts to access it.
findings.evidence: Set-Cookie: __Secure-STRP=ANmZwa1nNL5RpuTTU_hFun5tDaLezHbYBOE4AEB_F-zb2P2lW7nf74rKv4mvXzVMC9IPiAoSKDrCfAM-wxOr7o38Wnut_TSOVA; expires=Wed, 10-Jun-2026 10:39:40 GMT; path=/; domain=.google.com; Secure; SameSite=strict
findings.detectionLogic: Match httponly parameter in set-cookie headers
findings.aiAnalysis: The session cookie "__Secure-STRP" lacks HttpOnly. This is reported as a probable misconfiguration because while the flag is missing, session theft requires a secondary script injection (XSS) vulnerability to exploit.
findings.falsePositiveAssessment: Confirm if the cookie is indeed a session cookie. Since the flag is missing, the risk is real but conditional on XSS. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
findings.businessImpact: Enables session hijacking via XSS scripting.
findings.remediation: Add the 'HttpOnly' flag when configuring the cookie on the server.

findings.description: The cookie "__Secure-STRP" (Session Identifier) is not protected with the HttpOnly attribute, allowing client-side scripts to access it.
findings.reasoning: The session cookie "__Secure-STRP" lacks HttpOnly. This is reported as a probable misconfiguration because while the flag is missing, session theft requires a secondary script injection (XSS) vulnerability to exploit.

allFindings.title: Content-Security-Policy Header Omitted
allFindings.observation: No Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header was observed on the response.
allFindings.aiAnalysis: No CSP header was observed on the scanned response. However, no active XSS exploit vector was successfully demonstrated. The finding is treated as a best-practice hardening recommendation rather than evidence of exploitable risk.
allFindings.falsePositiveAssessment: A missing CSP header is a common observation. Modern client-side frameworks and CDNs may mitigate this. In the absence of demonstrated XSS, this remains a hardening advisory. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
allFindings.businessImpact: Lack of CSP increases the impact of future script injection (XSS) bugs, allowing session hijacking.
allFindings.remediation: Implement a strict Content-Security-Policy header, setting default-src to self and whitelisting trusted hosts.

allFindings.title: Missing HTTPS Encryption
allFindings.observation: The target application is hosted over HTTP. Traffic is transmitted in plain text, making it vulnerable to sniffing.
allFindings.detectionLogic: Check if URL scheme matches http:// and redirection is absent
allFindings.aiAnalysis: The target site accepts plain text connections and does not automatically redirect to HTTPS, leaving user traffic completely unencrypted and vulnerable to MITM attacks.
allFindings.falsePositiveAssessment: Verified that requests do not upgrade. This is a confirmed vulnerability for production applications. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Exploitability not demonstrated. Downgraded to Best Practice.]
allFindings.businessImpact: Severe risk of credential sniffing, session hijacking, and traffic tampering by network attackers.
allFindings.remediation: Configure the web server to enforce SSL/TLS and redirect all HTTP connections to HTTPS.
allFindings.description: The target application is hosted over HTTP. Traffic is transmitted in plain text, making it vulnerable to sniffing.
allFindings.reasoning: The target site accepts plain text connections and does not automatically redirect to HTTPS, leaving user traffic completely unencrypted and vulnerable to MITM attacks.

allFindings.title: Cookie Missing HttpOnly Flag: __Secure-STRP
allFindings.observation: The cookie "__Secure-STRP" (Session Identifier) is not protected with the HttpOnly attribute, allowing client-side scripts to access it.
allFindings.evidence: Set-Cookie: __Secure-STRP=ANmZwa1nNL5RpuTTU_hFun5tDaLezHbYBOE4AEB_F-zb2P2lW7nf74rKv4mvXzVMC9IPiAoSKDrCfAM-wxOr7o38Wnut_TSOVA; expires=Wed, 10-Jun-2026 10:39:40 GMT; path=/; domain=.google.com; Secure; SameSite=strict
allFindings.detectionLogic: Match httponly parameter in set-cookie headers
allFindings.aiAnalysis: The session cookie "__Secure-STRP" lacks HttpOnly. This is reported as a probable misconfiguration because while the flag is missing, session theft requires a secondary script injection (XSS) vulnerability to exploit.
allFindings.falsePositiveAssessment: Confirm if the cookie is indeed a session cookie. Since the flag is missing, the risk is real but conditional on XSS. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
allFindings.businessImpact: Enables session hijacking via XSS scripting.
allFindings.remediation: Add the 'HttpOnly' flag when configuring the cookie on the server.

allFindings.description: The cookie "__Secure-STRP" (Session Identifier) is not protected with the HttpOnly attribute, allowing client-side scripts to access it.
allFindings.reasoning: The session cookie "__Secure-STRP" lacks HttpOnly. This is reported as a probable misconfiguration because while the flag is missing, session theft requires a secondary script injection (XSS) vulnerability to exploit.

allFindings.description: The cookie "NID" (Preference/Analytics) is missing the SameSite flag, rendering users vulnerable to CSRF.
allFindings.reasoning: The cookie lacks SameSite. This is a low-risk hardening recommendation.

allFindings.title: Server Banner Disclosure: gws
allFindings.observation: The web server exposes its software type in the response headers.
allFindings.detectionLogic: Read Server header and check for version numbers
allFindings.aiAnalysis: Only the general server software name is disclosed, which is standard configuration disclosure.
allFindings.falsePositiveAssessment: Generic server type disclosure is a standard footprint. Low security impact. Note: Scanning detected security infrastructure (Google Web Server). Exploit paths are likely mitigated or blocked. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
allFindings.remediation: Configure the web server to disable or mask the Server response header (e.g. ServerTokens ProductOnly).
